form.thankYouMessage: Your responses have been saved. We will be in touch soon.
form.error: Failed to submit assessment. Please try again.

QO4.text: Where are you right now in your education or career?

QO5.text: If you are studying or have studied at university, what is your field?

QO6.text: Which sectors are you naturally drawn to? Pick up to 3.

QO7.text: How do you see your ideal career structure?

options.employee: Working within an organization (employee, manager, eventually leader)
options.founder: Building my own business or startup as my main work
options.blend: A blend — having a stable role AND a side business or independent project

QO8.text: What languages do you speak at home?

QO9.text: Are you open to building your career outside your home country?

options.no: No, I want to stay local

QO10.text: What brought you to this assessment today?

Q1.text: I enjoy working with tools, machines, or technology to build, fix, or operate things.

Q2.text: When facing a problem, I would rather try a hands-on solution than spend time analyzing or discussing it.

Q3.text: I am naturally drawn to understanding how and why things work — surface-level explanations do not satisfy me.

Q4.text: When I encounter a complex problem, my instinct is to research and analyze it before acting.

Q5.text: I feel most energized when I am creating something original — writing, designing, building, or performing.

Q6.text: Two work environments, one year each. Which would you choose?

options.A: A creative role with freedom to experiment and few rules.
options.B: A structured role with clear processes, defined outputs, and predictable rhythm.

Q7.text: I find it genuinely satisfying to help others solve their personal or professional challenges.

Q8.text: I am at my best when working closely with people — teaching, supporting, or coaching them — rather than working independently.

Q9.text: I enjoy persuading others to take action on my ideas or see things from my perspective.

Q10.text: I am drawn to roles where I can lead, influence decisions, or drive a project toward a goal.

Q11.text: I feel most productive when my work has clear structure, defined rules, and predictable processes.

Q12.text: I enjoy managing data, organizing information, or maintaining accurate records.

QFC_RI.text: You notice something at work is broken or not working well. Which feels more natural to you?

options.A: Roll up my sleeves and try to fix or build it myself.
options.B: Research what is causing it and understand the root problem.

QFC_SE.text: You are leading a team toward a tight deadline. Which feels more like you?

options.A: I focus on keeping the team motivated, supported, and working well together.
options.B: I focus on the goal — driving performance, making decisions, and winning.

Q13.text: I regularly seek out new ideas, experiences, or perspectives — even when they challenge what I already believe.

Q14.text: I tend to think about abstract possibilities and future concepts more than immediate, practical realities.

Q15.text: I plan ahead naturally and rarely leave important tasks to the last minute.

Q16.text: I hold myself to a high standard of work quality even when no one is checking.

Q17.text: You have a free evening after a demanding week. Which feels more appealing?

options.A: Going to a social gathering with friends or colleagues.
options.B: Spending the evening quietly at home alone or with close family.

Q18.text: In a group meeting or discussion, I tend to speak up early and share my views rather than listen first.

Q19.text: I tend to put other people's needs ahead of my own, even when I am already stretched.

Q20.text: I find it genuinely difficult to say no — even when taking on more would not be good for me.

Q21.text: I generally stay calm and composed when things go wrong or when I am under significant pressure.

Q22.text: After a setback or failure, I tend to recover relatively quickly rather than dwelling on it.

Q23.text: Predictable income and stable employment matter more to me than higher pay with risk or uncertainty.

Q24.text: I would turn down an exciting career opportunity if it came with significant financial uncertainty or job insecurity.

Q25.text: Setting my own schedule and working from wherever I choose is very important to my career satisfaction.

Q26.text: I would accept meaningfully lower pay in exchange for more control over how and when I work.

Q27.text: I want my work to contribute to something larger than my own career success or financial goals.

Q28.text: Knowing my work makes a real, visible difference to others gives me deep meaning and motivation.

Q29.text: The reputation and prestige of the organization I work for matters a great deal to me.

Q30.text: Being publicly recognized as an expert, leader, or high achiever in my field is one of my key motivations.

Q31.text: My family's opinion of my career choice plays an important role in the decisions I make.

Q32.text: I feel a strong sense of responsibility to support my family financially or professionally through my career.

Q33.text: I need my work to involve creating new things — new ideas, products, solutions, or experiences.

Q34.text: I would feel restless and unfulfilled in a role where I had to follow standard procedures with little room to shape my own approach.

Q35.text: Building significant personal wealth and financial security is one of my primary motivations.

Q36.text: You enjoy your work but receive a much higher-paying offer in a less interesting field. Which is most like you?

options.A: I would take the higher pay — financial security and growth come first.
options.B: I would stay where I am fulfilled — money matters but is not the deciding factor.

Q37.text: I want my work to be part of building my country's future — not only advancing my own career.

Q38.text: Two equally good job offers come your way. Which feels more like you?

options.A: A role with a local organization contributing directly to a major national project.
options.B: A role with a global multinational that gives international exposure and faster external career growth.

Q39.text: How my family, friends, and community see me professionally matters deeply — I want to be someone they can speak well of.

Q40.text: Two roles with similar pay. Which would you choose?

options.A: A respected, well-known organization everyone in your community recognizes by name.
options.B: A lesser-known but highly innovative organization where you would have more impact and growth, but less external recognition.

Q41.text: When approaching a problem, I naturally think several steps ahead and consider how scenarios might play out.

Q42.text: I am often the person in a group who notices patterns or connections others have overlooked.

Q43.text: Others naturally turn to me for direction or guidance when a group needs to decide what to do next.

Q44.text: I feel a genuine sense of responsibility for the performance and wellbeing of the people around me.

Q45.text: I invest significant time and energy in building deep, long-term relationships with the people I work with.

Q46.text: I am genuinely curious about the people I meet — their stories, motivations, what makes them who they are.

Q47.text: I am not satisfied until a task is fully completed to a high standard — starting something is never enough.

Q48.text: I follow through on what I commit to, even when circumstances make it difficult or inconvenient.

Q49.text: I find it natural to explain complex ideas in a simple, engaging way that motivates or inspires others.

Q50.text: I enjoy presenting, writing, or speaking — I like the process of turning ideas into compelling narratives.

Q51.text: I am constantly seeking to develop new skills and knowledge, even outside my formal role or field of study.

Q52.text: I find the process of learning something new genuinely enjoyable — even when the subject is difficult or unfamiliar.

Q53.text: I have a clear sense of where I want to be in my career in 5–10 years, and I am actively working toward it.

Q54.text: I have maintained focus on the same major interest, goal, or direction for several years — even when other things competed for my attention.

Q55.text: When I encounter a significant setback or failure, I eventually find a way to push forward rather than giving up.

Q56.text: I am willing to work very hard for a long time toward something I believe in, even without immediate reward or recognition.

Q57.text: Finishing what I start — even when it becomes tedious, difficult, or takes longer than expected — is something I pride myself on.

Q58.text: When I fail at something important, my instinct is to understand what went wrong and try a different approach rather than moving on.

Q59.text: Three months into a job, your manager tells you firmly that a project you worked hard on needs to be redone. Which feels most like you?

options.A: Feel demotivated for days and start wondering if this job is right for me.
options.B: Feel disappointed in the moment, then dig in to understand what specifically needs to change.

Q60.text: You have had a hard week — long hours, your boss spoke to you sharply about a missed deadline, and you are tired. It is Friday evening and you are thinking about Monday. Which feels closest to your honest reaction?

options.A: Wondering if this job is worth it — life is short.
options.B: Annoyed but planning to be back on Monday and keep going.
options.C: Already thinking about how to have a calm conversation with my boss about it.

Q61.text: Your first or next full-time job, six months in. The work has turned out to be harder and less interesting than you expected. Which is most like you?

options.A: Start looking for something new immediately.
options.B: Give it 6–12 more months to build skills and network before deciding.
options.C: Try to renegotiate my role with my manager first.
options.D: Stick with it for at least 2 years for the experience value.

Q64.text: Two friends are talking about their jobs. Which view feels closer to yours?

options.A: I would never let a job affect my mental health — I would quit tomorrow.
options.B: You cannot quit every time something is hard — that is how you build resilience.
options.C: Somewhere between — depends on how bad it really is.

Q65.text: Two work environments. Which feels more like you?

options.A: Fast-changing, high-intensity environment where priorities shift weekly and you are often improvising.
options.B: Steady, well-paced environment with clear priorities and predictable rhythms.

Q66.text: Where would you most thrive professionally?

options.A: A large, established organization with clear structure, established processes, and a recognizable name.
options.B: A small, scrappy organization where everyone wears many hats and you can shape how things work.

Q67.text: If you had to pick the work setting that genuinely fits you best, which one?

options.A: A government or government-linked organization, with the stability and national-mission framing that comes with it.
options.B: A private-sector company — corporate, startup, or family business — with more variability and direct market exposure.

Q68.text: I would find it energizing to spend a meaningful chunk of my career working in different cities or countries.

Q69.text: I have previously started, co-founded, or seriously developed a business idea, freelance venture, or independent project.

Q71.text: Two career paths. Which feels more like you?

options.A: A path where you would build something of your own — possibly higher reward, definitely higher uncertainty.
options.B: A path where you would grow within a strong organization — more predictable trajectory, more support, less personal risk.

Q73.text: The idea of building something of my own — whether as my main career or alongside a stable job — energizes me, even if it means working harder.
